senses.definition: A chemical, such as sodium thiosulfate, used to remove excess chlorine from cloth or other materials after bleaching.
examples: An antichlor is added after bleaching to remove any remaining chlorine from the fabric.
The technician used an antichlor to neutralize the chlorine in the solution.

senses.definition: A substance used to remove or neutralize chlorine, especially after bleaching or disinfection.
examples: An antichlorine is required to treat the water before it is discharged.
The antichlorine quickly reduced the chlorine level in the pool.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the effect of removing or neutralizing chlorine.
examples: The antichloristic action of the compound protects the fibers from damage.
These antichloristic additives are essential in modern bleaching processes.

senses.definition: Preventing or treating chlorosis, especially in plants or in human blood.
examples: An antichlorotic spray was applied to the affected leaves.
The doctor prescribed an antichlorotic tonic for her anemia.

senses.definition: Plural of antichlor: chemicals used to remove excess chlorine from materials after bleaching.
examples: Different antichlors are available for various industrial processes.
These antichlors are added at the end of the washing cycle.

senses.definition: Opposing the legal right of a woman to choose to have an abortion.
examples: The senator is known for his antichoice voting record.
Antichoice groups organized a protest outside the clinic.

senses.definition: A person or group that opposes the legal right to abortion.
examples: An antichoice argued against the new reproductive health bill.
The panel included both pro-choice advocates and antichoicers.

senses.definition: A person who is opposed to the legal right to abortion.
examples: As an antichoicer, she campaigns to restrict abortion access.
Antichoicers and pro-choice activists often clash at rallies.

senses.definition: People who are opposed to the legal right to abortion.
examples: Antichoicers gathered to demonstrate against the court ruling.
Lawmakers heard from both antichoicers and pro-choice advocates.

senses.definition: A drug or substance that reduces the flow of bile or suppresses bile secretion.
examples: The physician prescribed an anticholagogue to manage the patient’s symptoms.
This herb was traditionally used as an anticholagogue.

senses.definition: Designed to lower or control the level of cholesterol in the blood.
examples: He follows an anticholesterol diet rich in vegetables and whole grains.
Anticholesterol drugs are commonly prescribed to reduce heart disease risk.

senses.definition: Blocking the action of acetylcholine in the nervous system or at nerve endings.
examples: The patient experienced dry mouth as a side effect of the anticholinergic drug.
Anticholinergic properties can cause confusion in older adults.

senses.definition: A drug that blocks the action of acetylcholine, often used to treat conditions like asthma, incontinence, or motion sickness.
examples: The doctor prescribed an anticholinergic to relieve his stomach cramps.
Some anticholinergics are used to treat overactive bladder.

senses.definition: Drugs that block the action of acetylcholine in the nervous system.
examples: Anticholinergics are commonly used to treat conditions like asthma and overactive bladder.
Long-term use of anticholinergics may increase the risk of cognitive decline.

senses.definition: A substance that inhibits the enzyme cholinesterase and so increases the level of acetylcholine in the body.
examples: Pyridostigmine is an anticholinesterase used to improve muscle strength in myasthenia gravis.
The researchers tested a new anticholinesterase for treating dementia.

senses.definition: Substances that inhibit the enzyme cholinesterase, often used as drugs or toxins.
examples: Some anticholinesterases are used as insecticides in agriculture.
Different anticholinesterases were compared in the clinical trial.

senses.definition: Opposed to romantic love or strongly critical of romantic ideals and stories.
examples: Her antichoromanic essays challenged traditional love narratives.
The film takes an antichoromanic view of relationships.

senses.definition: A group or voice that opposes or answers the main chorus, especially in drama or poetry.
examples: The playwright used an antichorus to question the main characters’ decisions.
The antichorus provides a critical counterpoint to the joyful chorus.

senses.definition: Plural of antichresis: contracts in which a borrower gives a lender the right to use land or property and take its income instead of paying interest.
examples: In some legal systems, antichreses are still recognized as valid security agreements.
The lawyer explained how antichreses work in property law.

senses.definition: A legal agreement where a borrower gives a lender the right to use land or property and receive its profits instead of paying interest on a loan.
examples: Under antichresis, the creditor may occupy the land until the debt is paid.
Antichresis was more common in older civil law traditions.

senses.definition: Relating to antichresis, where property is used by a creditor instead of receiving interest payments.
examples: The antichretic arrangement allowed the lender to farm the land.
They entered into an antichretic contract under local civil law.

senses.definition: In Christian belief, a powerful figure who is completely against Christ and is expected to appear before the end of the world.
examples: Some Christians believe the Antichrist will deceive many people.
The novel imagines a political leader as the Antichrist.

senses.definition: A person who is seen as completely against Christian values or as very evil.
examples: In the speech, he was portrayed as an Antichrist destroying moral values.
Some critics called the dictator an Antichrist of his time.

senses.definition: Strongly opposed to Christian beliefs, values, or influence.
examples: They protested what they saw as antichristian laws.
The book was banned for its antichristian ideas.

senses.definition: Strong opposition to Christianity as a religion or to Christian influence in society.
examples: The author examines the roots of antichristianism in modern culture.
Antichristianism was a key theme in their political program.

senses.definition: Strong rejection of Christianity or active resistance to Christian beliefs and institutions.
examples: The regime’s antichristianity led to the closure of many churches.
Her writings explore the growth of antichristianity in secular societies.

senses.definition: In a way that strongly opposes Christian beliefs or values.
examples: The policy was seen as acting antichristianly toward the church.
He wrote antichristianly about religious traditions.

senses.definition: Plural of Antichrist: figures or people who are seen as strongly against Christ or Christian values.
examples: Some texts warn believers about false prophets and antichrists.
In popular culture, various villains are described as antichrists.

senses.definition: Resistant to the effects of chromic acid or chromium compounds, especially in dyeing or tanning.
examples: Antichrome dyes are used for special industrial fabrics.
The antichrome leather kept its color despite harsh treatment.

senses.definition: Not following the normal order of time; out of chronological sequence.
examples: The novel has an antichronical structure, jumping between centuries.
An antichronical timeline can confuse some readers.

senses.definition: In a way that does not follow normal time order.
examples: The events are presented antichronically throughout the film.
The story moves antichronically between the hero’s childhood and old age.

senses.definition: A lack of normal time order or a deliberate mixing of different times in a text or work of art.
examples: The director’s use of antichronism gives the film a dreamlike quality.
Antichronism is common in postmodern narratives.

senses.definition: In ancient Greek astronomy, a supposed planet or counter-Earth on the opposite side of the sun from Earth.
examples: The philosopher speculated about the motion of the antichthon.
In some Pythagorean systems, the antichthon helped explain eclipses.

senses.definition: People supposed to live on the opposite side of the Earth, unseen by those in the known world.
examples: Medieval maps sometimes included regions for the antichthones.
Legends described the antichthones as living under another sun.

senses.definition: Opposed to the influence, power, or teachings of the Christian church.
examples: The party adopted an antichurch platform.
Antichurch policies led to the closure of several monasteries.

senses.definition: A movement or group that strongly opposes the Christian church.
examples: The antichurch gained support among urban intellectuals.
He joined an antichurch that rejected traditional religious authority.

senses.definition: Hostile to or strongly critical of church institutions or church influence.
examples: His antichurchian writings attacked institutional religion.
The movement took an antichurchian stance on education.

senses.definition: An antibody or substance that neutralizes chymosin, an enzyme used in cheese-making.
examples: Antichymosin antibodies can affect cheese production.
The presence of antichymosin reduced the enzyme’s activity.

senses.definition: Opposed to smoking cigarettes or to the cigarette industry.
examples: There has been a strong anticigarette campaign in recent years.
Anticigarette laws have reduced smoking in public places.

senses.definition: A person who anticipates or expects something to happen.
examples: Each anticipant waited nervously for the results.
As an anticipant of change, she had already prepared a new plan.

senses.definition: Showing or expressing expectation of something.
examples: He gave her an anticipant smile.
There was an anticipant silence in the hall.

senses.definition: People who anticipate or expect something to happen.
examples: The anticipants gathered outside the hall, waiting for the announcement.
All anticipants of the prize were asked to sit in the front row.

senses.definition: Able to be predicted or expected in advance.
examples: Most of the costs were anticipatable and appeared in the budget.
The risks are anticipatable and can be managed.

senses.definition: To expect something to happen and often prepare for it.
examples: We anticipate increased demand for online services this year.
She did not anticipate such a strong reaction from the audience.

senses.definition: To do or say something before another person or event, often to prevent or influence it.
examples: The company anticipated criticism by improving its safety standards.
To anticipate a question, yes, we have enough funds.

senses.definition: Expected or planned as likely to happen.
examples: The anticipated result of the study was confirmed.
Sales were lower than anticipated in the first quarter.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of anticipate: expected something to happen and often prepared for it.
examples: We anticipated several challenges before launching the project.
They had anticipated that costs would rise.

senses.definition: Third person singular of anticipate: expects something to happen and often prepares for it.
examples: The company anticipates strong growth next year.
She anticipates that the meeting will be short.

senses.definition: Present participle of anticipate: expecting something to happen and often preparing for it.
examples: We are anticipating an increase in applications.
Anticipating trouble, they left early.

senses.definition: Feeling excited or nervous while expecting something to happen.
examples: The anticipating crowd waited outside the stadium.
An anticipating smile spread across his face.

senses.definition: In a way that shows you are expecting something to happen.
examples: She looked at him anticipatingly, waiting for his answer.
The audience leaned forward anticipatingly before the final note.

senses.definition: A feeling of excitement or nervousness about something that is going to happen.
examples: They waited in great anticipation for the results.
The children’s faces were bright with anticipation.

senses.definition: The act of expecting something and preparing for it.
examples: In anticipation of higher demand, the company increased production.
The budget includes extra funds in anticipation of future costs.

senses.definition: Plural of anticipation: feelings or acts of expecting and preparing for future events.
examples: Their anticipations of success were high.
Different anticipations about the market led to different strategies.

senses.definition: Showing the ability or tendency to anticipate or act before something happens.
examples: An anticipative approach can prevent many problems.
The manager praised her anticipative thinking.

senses.definition: In a way that involves acting before something happens, based on what you expect.
examples: We must act anticipatively to avoid a crisis.
She anticipatively adjusted the plan to meet future needs.

senses.definition: A person who anticipates or predicts things before they happen.
examples: As an anticipator of trends, she invested early in renewable energy.
Good leaders are anticipators of potential problems.

senses.definition: A device or mechanism that reacts before a change fully happens, usually to control a system.
examples: The thermostat includes an anticipator to reduce temperature swings.
Adjusting the anticipator can improve heating efficiency.

senses.definition: In a way that anticipates or happens before something else, often to influence it.
examples: The company acted anticipatorily by cutting costs before the downturn.
She answered anticipatorily, before he had finished the question.

senses.definition: People or devices that anticipate future events or changes.
examples: Successful investors are skilled anticipators of market shifts.
Modern thermostats act as anticipators to keep rooms comfortable.
